deck: Part three of Dimagi's AI series. Jonathan Jackson and research lead Brian DeRenzi catch up on a fast-moving field, from low-resource language testing to Open Chat Studio, and how to bend the arc of AI toward equity.
seo_description: Jonathan Jackson and Brian DeRenzi on equity-enhancing AI use cases, low-resource languages, and Dimagi's Open Chat Studio platform for building chatbots.
og_description: Dimagi's AI series, part 3: equity-enhancing use cases, low-resource languages, and Open Chat Studio.
about: In part three of Dimagi's series on artificial intelligence, Jonathan Jackson sits down with Brian DeRenzi, Dimagi's Research and Data team lead, to take stock of how fast the field has moved in the six months since their last conversation. They discuss the rapid pace of foundational model releases, why the improvements are shifting rather than slowing, and what all of this means for global health.

The conversation centers on how AI can be leveraged to improve health outcomes, support frontline health workers rather than replace them, and ensure equitable access to these tools. Brian and Jon walk through three areas of work, direct-to-client uses, coaching for health workers, and program manager assistants, alongside Dimagi's push into low-resource languages and open source models. They also dig into Open Chat Studio, Dimagi's platform for quickly building, testing, and deploying chatbots, and the harder strategic question of where AI fits inside CommCare, SureAdhere, and Dimagi's other products.

pull_quote.text: At the most fundamental level, if these models don't speak your language, you're not going to get any benefits from AI.

bullets: Why AI isn't really slowing down: four major model releases in a few months, and why the improvements are shifting rather than stalling
Bending the arc toward equity: the case for racing toward AI that works for the people the market won't serve on its own
Low-resource languages: testing a dozen languages in a few hours, from Zulu to Chichewa to Wolof, and the challenge of Sheng in Kenya
Open Chat Studio: Dimagi's platform for building, testing, and deploying chatbots, designed so non-developers can use it too
Three use case buckets: direct-to-client, coaching for health workers, and a new program manager assistant
AI inside the products: table stakes features, add-ons, and net-new capabilities for CommCare and SureAdhere, plus voice interfaces
The 70 percent question: avoiding a dystopia where only the wealthy reach a real health worker and everyone else gets a good-enough bot

stats.label: of Dimagi experience grounding its work on equitable AI
languages the team tested on a model in a single afternoon
cost of consumer ChatGPT access, used to frame how AI pricing may evolve

guests.name: Brian DeRenzi
guests.role: Research and Data team lead, leading much of Dimagi's AI work, including evidence generation, low-resource language research, and Open Chat Studio.
